author: Murphy
summary: Anti-submarine nets (aka anti-torpedo nets or torpedo nets) are steel-wire or ring-mesh barriers that were bung from buoys or booms. The nets stretched across harbor entrances or around ships to physically block torpedoes or submarines because they reached the respective targets. These nets emerged in the late 19th century in response to the first self propelled torpedo, the Whitehead. Experiments began in 1877 with HMS Thunderer (Royal Navy ship) was the first ship to be fitted with the nets (Wiki) The nets were in common naval use from the 1890s through WW1 and WW2. For the BOF the nets mattered as a harbor defense system and in the technology that would later lead to submarine detectors. The Board’s focus, as stated on the 51st Congress Session of 1891, is to purchase, experiment and test equipment like “torpedoes… and other implements and engines of war” (51st Congress). Based on this harbor defenses and anti-submarine measures fell within its range, even though most of its practical development of nets migrated to the Navy’s Bureau of Ordnance and specialized “Net and Boom Defense” orgs by WW1 (Bureau of Ordnance Publication). These nets were superseded by anti-torpedo bulges (Wiki). (1905 BOF Report mentions submarine mines)

author: Kim
source_file: BOF Technology Timeline Memo – Automobile Dirigible Torpedoes(Howell Torpedoes) - Kim.docx
summary: The Howell Automobile Torpedo was the first U.S.-designed and manufactured self-propelled torpedo adopted by the U.S. Navy. Invented by Lieutenant Commander John A. Howell in 1871, it was created in response to England’s adoption of Robert Whitehead’s torpedo in 1866. Howell’s design was unique because it used a massive, pre-spun flywheel for propulsion, rather than compressed air. This flywheel provided energy for propulsion and gyroscopic stability, making the weapon wakeless and accurate. Additionally, the Howell had an uncomplicated design that made the torpedo cheaper to build. It was manufactured for the Navy starting in 1889 by the Hotchkiss Ordnance Company. Despite its reliability, the Howell was discontinued by the Navy around 1898. Its limited speed and range could not compete once the Whitehead adopted its own gyroscope and improved its compressed-air power system, making the Howell obsolete.

body_sections.heading: Timeline of Development & BOF Excerpts
lines: A stitched chronology combining BOF excerpts and context from other sources. Feel free to basically excerpt all complete mentions from the BOF annual reports, and then “fill in the blanks” of additional context as necessary with external sources. This should be the most detailed part of this memo. The entries can be long!
● 1866, Robert Whitehead develops the first mobile torpedo model

○ Robert Whitehead develops the first experimental model of mobile torpedo. His torpedo design became the global standard for the weapon. The model had several limitations namely its limited range of 200 yards, lack of directional stability due to the lack of sophisticated stabilizing mechanism, and the wake of the torpedo being visible when in use.
○ Lieutenant Commander John A. Howell filed the original patent (US121052A) for a torpedo propelled and stabilized by a flywheel. The flywheel was spun up prior to launch

● 1883, US Congress opens Torpedo Competition for the prize of $100,000 ○ Extensive testing of prototypes occurred in the 1883 competition. Howell competed against two surface-running, rocket-propelled torpedoes created by the American Torpedo company and Asa Weeks. The Howell torpedo was the only design recommended for service by the Navy's Torpedo Board

● 1888-10-30, Board of Ordnance orders Howell torpedoes for further test trials ○ “The moveable torpedo under control from shore stations is an offensive weapon the powers of which are not yet fully developed. It is of limited range, of uncertain accuracy, and of so complex mechanism as to be liable to frequent failures. Its true function is the defense of mine fields; and for this purpose it appears worthy of the development, bearing in mind, however, that it can never constitute more than auxiliary defense.”(24, BOF.AR.1.1888-1891)

● 1895, Whitehead incorporates Obry Gyroscope into his torpedo design ○ The Whitehead torpedo was fitted with the Obry Gyroscope. The Obry Gyroscope was developed by Austrian naval officer and engineer Ludwig Obry. This advancement eliminated Howell's unique advantage in stability, as the Whitehead could now also maintain its course reliably. Howell sued Whitehead in response for patent infringement as he believed the Obry Gyroscope infringed his flywheel design.
● 1898, Howell Torpedo officially discontinued by the Navy

○ Howell torpedoes were deemed obsolete and discontinued by the Navy shortly after the Spanish-American War. The Whitehead/Bliss-Leavitt designs, powered by more effective compressed-air engines and offered superior speed and range. ■ Source: https://navalunderseamuseum.org/howell/

body_sections.heading: Invention Assessment
lines: Answer as many of the following questions to the best of your ability in a few sentences. Can keep them as a bulleted list!
● Why was there interest in this technology?

○ The Navy had intense interest because Howell’s design solved the major problem of directional stability that plagued early torpedoes, using the simple gyroscopic effect of the flywheel. It also offered a unique wakeless (silent) advantage, as it had no compressed air exhaust.
● What other technologies were related to this one (did it competed with any or complement or improve upon other technologies)?
○ The Howell competed directly with the Whitehead torpedo. The competition effectively forced the Whitehead design to adopt the gyroscope, which in turn pushed Howell’s design out of commission.
● Efficacy or shortcomings based on trials/data?
○ The Howell was highly accurate and maintained its course and depth reliably. Its major shortcomings were its limited speed and range compared to the rapidly evolving, air-powered Whitehead torpedo.
○ The Howell provided great accuracy and stability but at the cost of speed and range. Its manufacturing process was also complex due to the flywheel
mechanics and the need for external spool-up equipment. Despite its complex manufacturing process, its manufacturing costs itself were relatively low
compared to the production of torpedoes.
○ The Navy initially funded and adopted the Howell due to its unique gyroscopic stability and reliability. It was later discontinued because the competing
Whitehead design, with the addition of a gyroscope and heated compressed air, achieved far greater speed and range, making the Howell obsolete.

● Where did it go in the War Department after it was funded by the BOF, e.g. did the Army or Navy end up buying this technology at scale? Using this technology? ○ The Navy, concerned about falling behind foreign navies, contracted for 100 Whitehead torpedoes in 1892. The Howell torpedo's power source—the
flywheel—could not compete with the rapidly improving performance of the compressed-air powered Whitehead.

○ The development of the Howell helped lay the groundwork for future U.S. Navy testing standards and requirements for complex weapons systems. The ultimate failure to keep pace with foreign development demonstrated the need for proactive and consistent guidance in technological capabilities and ordnance development.

body_sections.heading: Details on Relevant People/Organizations
lines: Any additional details/elements if necessary if there are interesting personalities/human issues that come up in the context of this technology’s story.

● Robert Whitehead: British inventor whose torpedo design (invented in 1866) established the global standard for the weapon, making him the chief rival to Howell. The Howell torpedo was an explicit American response to Whitehead's technological dominance. Crucially, Whitehead's continued improvements, particularly the adoption of the Obry

Gyroscope in 1895, directly negated Howell's main technical advantage—stability. By the late 1890s, the superior speed and range of the compressed-air Whitehead designs led the U.S. Navy to discontinue the Howell in their favor.
● Hotchkiss Ordnance Company: The Hotchkiss Ordnance Company was a major international arms manufacturer that was critical to the Howell torpedo's success in U.S.

service. The company secured the rights to produce the torpedo after purchasing the patent from Howell in 1888. Hotchkiss received the initial U.S. Navy contract for 50 Howell torpedoes in 1889. Their involvement was essential in transitioning Howell's design from an innovative concept into a manufactured, fleet-ready weapon, solidifying their status as a leading domestic ordnance producer.

● John A. Howell: Howell was motivated to provide the U.S. Navy with a reliable, domestically produced torpedo after observing the success of the foreign-made Whitehead. Howell's unique design featured a flywheel that served both as the propulsion source and to generate a stabilizing gyroscopic effect, solving the directional problems that plagued earlier torpedoes. His later patent litigation against Robert Whitehead highlights his deep commitment to his intellectual property.

summary: The Brown segmental-wire gun was one of the first projects of the BOF, mentioned in the very first (1888-1891) Board report to Congress. The gun was a piece of naval artillery, which used the novel technology of using wiring to stabilize the firing barrel. Reportedly, this drastically increased the gun's dexterity and stability when firing and the board initially viewed the gun with optimism. However, between 1894-1907, the project got bogged down in repairs, malfunctions, and delays until it was finally canceled in 1907 and the Crozier gun was chosen as the better model. The design was never used again and wire-wound artillery ultimately died-out altogether.

body_sections.heading: Timeline of Development & BOF Excerpts
lines: 1890/1892- First mentions of brown gun (5-inch model) in BOF reports

BOF Excerpt #1: “The Board has made an allotment of $3,031 for the purchase of ammunition for the test of the Brown segmental wire-wound gun, and has also made an allotment for adapting a carriage for proving or testing the same.” (1888-1891 Report, pp. 20)

BOF Excerpt #2: “an experimental gun built by a private company, but to be tested by the Board, for which purpose an allotment tor ammunition and for the preparation of a carriage has been made, is now nearing completion and will soon be ready for test. The gun is, as its name implies, constructed with a tube composed of longitudinal segments, and these segments closely fitted to each other and wound with steel wire. It is proposed to line the segmental tube throughout the reinforcements, and if needful throughout its entire length, with a steel lining tube, as is understood, for the double purpose of preventing erosion in the joints of the segments and to strengthen the construction. The gun now being constructed is a 5-inch B. L. rifle, with a modification of the usual breech mechanism. A program for testing it to the extent of the first hundred rounds has been proposed, and it is expected that by the end of the present calendar year the test will have been commenced and perhaps completed. The gun is claimed to be capable of withstanding.” (1891-1892 Report pp. 10-11)

Additional Context: Mentioned in 1891 New York Times article as “gun of the future”. The article also describes ongoing testing at Fort Wadsworth, where the gun reportedly withstood barrel pressure of 58,200 pounds to square inch (which is not a measurement of pressure but force), which was viewed as very impressive and the main advantage of the gun. (https://timesmachine.nytimes.com/timesmachine/1891/04/14/103302340.html?pageNumber=9)

Analysis:  The gun, which at this point wasn't yet the 10-inch but the 5-inch model, was at this point little less than a prototype and the fact that it was both picked up by the Board and mentioned in the NYT shows that it was relatively well-known and viewed as having a lot of potential. Its barrel stability and endurance seems to have been the main attraction. It had already been tested in 1891 and found promising. It is important to note that the British were developing similar wire-wound guns for their Navy at about the same time.
1893- Testing begins at Sandy Hook (5-inch model)

BOF Excerpt: “This gun, constructed by a private corporation but mounted and to be tested under allotments made by the Board, was completed during the year, and is now at the ordnance proving ground at Sandy Hook undergoing test. The results thus far are understood to have been satisfactory. The gun is of 5 inches caliber and about 44 calibers in length. It is fitted with a special breech mechanism, the screw being continuous and requiring four revolutions of the handle. It has withstood some very heavy pressures and given some unusually high velocities. With a powder charge of 21 pounds of Leonard’s smokeless powder, and a projectile of 60 pounds, a velocity of 2,874 foot-seconds was attained with a pressure of 46,600 pounds per square inch. It has been fired to date about 100 rounds, of which 79 rounds have been fired at the proving ground with pressures of from 40,000 to 50,000 pounds to the inch.” (1892-1893 report, pp. 9)

Additional Context: In 1893, a Savannah Morning News journalist witnessed a public test of the gun at Birdsboro, PA. He was very impressed and noted the immense pressure the gun widthstew, calling them record-breaking. (https://gahistoricnewspapers.galileo.usg.edu/lccn/sn86063034/1893-04-16/ed-1/seq-1)

Additional Context: In 1896, The gun was featured in the Scientific American, a prestigious contemporary engineering journal, it showcased the gun's technical specifics and even features a blueprint of the gun. Importantly, the report viewed the gun with much optimism and gave it a full-page article.

Analysis: The Board conducted many tests at multiple locations. The Birdsboro test is noteworthy, since it was the first public test of gun, meaning that the board saw potential enough in it to market it to the public. The gun seems to have met enthusiasm from both the Board and the public, being viewed as a marvel of engineering for the pressure it was capable of withstanding. The board continued to test the gun and viewed the progress as satisfactory. The special mechanism is important, as it later caused many problems for the gun and repeatedly broke down.
1894-1901 First break-down and new model (5 & 10-inch model)

BOF Excerpt: “of which a full account was given in the last annual report, and which had then been fired about 100 rounds, was tested to the one hundred and ninety-second round, when the gun failed, having become unserviceable…, of which a full account was given in the last annual report, and which had then been fired about 100 rounds, was tested to the one hundred and ninety-second round, when the gun failed, having become unserviceable.” (1893-1894 report, pp. 6-7)

BOF Excerpt: “For these reasons the Board decided to recommend the allotment of $33,000 for the construction of a 10-inch rifle, and the recommendation was approved by the Secretary of War. The Trustees of the gun report that the mill for hot rolling the segments and the machine for winding the wire on the gun with the requisite initial tension are completed and that the wire and steel for the segments have been made. The lining tube is under construction by the Bethlehem Iron Company.” (1896-1897 report, pp. 9)

BOF Excerpt: “The Secretary of War approved the recommendation of the Board, and the gun was contracted for. On December 22, 1899, the trustees having reported the completion of the gun, the Board made a visit to the shops of the Reading Iron Company at Reading, Pa., for the purpose of inspecting the gun and the machinery used in its manufacture. The gun was shipped to the proving ground at Sandy Hook, New Jersey, on January 9, 1900, but a programme for its test has not yet been completed, owing to delay on the part of the trustees in designating a suitable powder. Recent correspondence, however, with the trustees indicates that a suitable powder will be determined upon at an early date.” (1899-1900 Report, pp. 6-7)

Additional Context: Century Magazine featured the Brown gun in a 1895 article, “NEW WEAPONS OF THE UNITED STATES ARMY”, which also featured many other Board artillery projects. It also alludes to the gun's rivalry with the Crozier wire-wound gun, which the article states is the only successfully tested version. (https://www.victorianvoices.net/ARTICLES/CENTURY/Century1895A/C1895A-Weapons.pdf) (pp. 577)

Analysis: After the 5-inch gun broke down during testing in 1894, after continuously firing 192 rounds. Nonetheless, the board was impressed by this performance and noted that it had withstood unprecedented pressure. Therefore, in 1896, the Board allocated $33,000, a huge sum, to construct a 10-inch version of the gun. It took Brown four years to reconstruct the gun, and it was only delivered to Sandy Hook in 1900, a considerable delay. The reason for this delay is unknown. Testing only resumed in February, 1901, amounting to a 5 year delay. The gun, already being considered by the board for 10 years, was now getting progressively more outdated. This may have been one of the main reasons for the gun not being adopted. It is also important to note that the gun was already being unfavorably compared with the Crozier model as early as 1895, 12 years before it was finally rejected.

BOF Excerpt: ​​”The first proof round, with a pressure of 10,000 pounds, developed a crack extending thru the third chase hoop. As this hoop is a thin covering used to protect the wire from injury, it was not believed that the defect had in any way weakened the gun, and on March 6, in the presence of the Board, three additional rounds were fired, with pressures ranging from 15,700 to 26,900 pounds. It being evident from these and subsequent rounds that with the special kind of powder determined upon the required velocities could not be obtained, the Board, on April 6, 1901, granted the request of the trustees to increase the chamber capacity of the gun. This alteration necessitated sending the gun to the shops of the manufacturer. When returned, it was again mounted and firing tests renewed on October 28, 1901. On the second round, with a charge of 175 pounds Du Pont smokeless powder for 12-inch rifle and a recorded pressure of 51,550 pounds per square-inch, the breech bushing, which was of cast steel and carried the breech block and mechanism, was torn from the gun and blown backward several hundred feet. On October 31, 1901, the Board inspected the gun and found that, except as above indicated, it was uninjured. Permission was granted to remove the gun for repairs, with the understanding that the Government was to be put to no expense therefore. August 12, 1902, the trustees again requested permission to further enlarge the chamber of the gun, and August 26 the Board granted this permission. In July, 1903, the gun was returned to the proving ground and mounted for continuation of test under a program of 100 rounds approved by the Board. July 30, 1903, one shot was fired in the presence of the Board with a charge of 100 pounds of smokeless powder. The breechblock was jammed by this shot and required considerable force to open it. Firings were continued from time to time until November 1903, the breech mechanism continuing to operate unsatisfactorily. On November 4, 1903, the company requested permission, which was granted, to substitute a new breech mechanism, and the gun was removed from the proving ground in January, 1905, for the purpose. It has not yet been returned. April 7, 1904, the company requested an additional allotment of $10,000 to cover extraordinary expenses incurred in the manufacture of the gun, but the request was not approved. Under date of July 2, 1906, the company stated that there was still a balance due the Reading Iron Company for repairs to the gun and requested an allotment to cover this and the cost of a new breechblock. The Board has not approved this request, and it is doubtful when the repairs will be completed.” (1905-1906 Report, pp. 5-6)

Analysis: After being built for 5 years, the gun again began testing at Sandy Hook, this time its performance was very unsatisfactory. The gun malfunctioned during every single test only after a few shots (or only one shot) were fired. The most serious malfunction occurred when, after only one shot, the entire breach bushing was torn-off and flew several hundred feet. The breach mechanism seems to have been the gun's biggest problem, as it repeatedly jammed even after this incident. In 1905, the Brown Company removed the gun from Sandy Hook in order to install a new breach mechanism. It was never returned. The considerable delay and severe underperformance of the gun raise many questions about what happened to the gun during its reconstruction period from 1894-1901.
1907- BOF chooses Crozier/rejects Brown gun (10-inch model)

BOF Excerpt: “The subject of wire-wound guns, which has been before the Board for several years, has finally been settled by the recommendation, ap- proved by the Secretary of War, that it is not advisable to adopt as a type the Brown segmental-tube wire-wound gun, nor to purchase or construct guns of this description for the service of the United States." (1907 - 1908 Report, pp. 4)

Additional Context: The Board finally decided to abandon the 10-inch model in 1907 after receiving requests from the Brown Wire Gun Company for more funding and further delays. By this time, there were also many other proposed experimental guns that covered the Brown gun's model, such as the Crozier wire-wound gun model, which was designed by the Chief of Ordnance.

body_sections.heading: Invention Assessment
lines: Why was there interest in this technology?

The gun was initially a very promising piece of naval artillery, able to withstand unprecedented pressure and maintain barrel stability while firing. It's initial tests were also very successful, the gun demonstrated its dexterity and only malfunctioned after almost 200 shots were fired consequently.
What other technologies were related to this one (did it compete with any or complement or improve upon other technologies)?

The Royal Navy was developing its own naval wire-wound artillery in the 1890s, the QF 6-inch naval gun (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QF_6-inch_naval_gun) and the BL 12-inch naval gun(https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BL_12-inch_Mk_VIII_naval_gun). Wire-wound technology was quite popular in the 1890s and the US Navy probably wanted to develop their own version and commissioned Brown for such a project. For most of its testing period, the gun competed with a model developed by William Crozier, which was chosen over the Brown gun in 1907, but was also never adopted. It's important to note that there were three different Brown gun models: the initial 5-inch model (1890-1894), a 10-inch model (1896-1907) and a 6-inch model (1902-1907), none of which were adopted.
Efficacy or shortcomings based on trials/data?

According to the BOF reports, the 10-inch model seems to have been poorly built, malfunctioning during every single test, even critically, when an ignition tore-off its entire breach mechanism. These reports show that the although the gun seems to have been designed well, the actual construction had serious problems that led to it eventually being rejected.
The gun repeatedly demonstrated superior capability in barrel stability and pressure resistance, however, it was bogged down by constant malfunctions and delays after 1894.
Continuous malfunctions and repeated failure to fix the problems the gun had.
Where did it go in the War Department after it was funded by the BOF, e.g. did the Army or Navy end up buying this technology at scale? Using this technology?
According to all available evidence, the gun was completely abandoned after it was rejected by the board.
The Crozier gun was adopted instead of the Brown gun, however, strangely even the Crozier design never entered service. The US never adopted wire-wound technology for its naval or ground forces.

body_sections.heading: Details on Relevant People/Organizations
lines: -Brown became a gunsmith and developed the “J.H. Brown rifle” in 1882, (https://ogca.com/the-nra-creedmoor-the-international-matches-and-the-j-h-brown-rifle-by-sean-a-mccarter/)
-The US shooting team used the Rifle for their 1883 match with Great Britain,
-Brown was a member of this team and took part in the competition,
-At this time, Brown was also a director of the National Rifle Association,
-Brown was relatively well-known by the time he began working with the Board in 1890.

-In 1903, Brown's wife assaulted a real-estate agent and was sent to jail before being bailed out by her husband, the incident was featured in the New York Times, (https://www.nytimes.com/1903/03/11/archives/investors-wife-arrested-mrs-john-hamilton-brown-accused-of-assault.html)
-He seems to have ended collaboration with the Board in 1915 and retired,

body_sections.heading: Open Questions & Next Steps
lines: How important was the rivalry with the Crozier Model to the Brown gun's dismissal?
What caused the gun's abysmal performance after 1901?
Did the Brown Company go through some internal crisis that caused the gun's delay?
Was there embezzlement involved on the part of board members or the Brown Company?
Did Crozier use his influence within the Board to promote his own model at the expense of the Brown Gun?
Who were the “trustees” of the Brown Gun Company?
Did the failure of the Brown Gun cause Brown to testify against Crozier in Congress?

The Brown Company explicitly protested the 1907 dismissal of both the 6-inch and 10-inch model in favor of the Crozier model, on the basis that the Crozier gun was a later model and it was therefore unfair to compare the two models. It's important to note that Crozier was the US Chief of Ordnance and member of the Board. Although he excused himself during deliberations on the Crozier gun, considering the explicit protest the Brown company leveled against the decision and the decision's devastating impact on the company, it's likely that Brown felt a personal vendetta against Crozier. Although Brown continued to work with the Board, his next project was much less ambitious. From 1909-1911, Brown worked on a method to prevent “gun erosion”, after which he took 3 years to “analyze” the results of his work, after which he disappeared from Board reports. Overall, the decision to choose the Crozier model effectively ended Brown's career and its likely that gave Brown a strong reason to testify against Crozier.

author: Stoddard
source_file: BOF Tech Memo_Lewis range and position finder_Stoddard.docx
summary: ​​The Lewis range finder, a vertical-base depression instrument, measured the range and direction of moving targets as part of the updates to coastal defense in the Endicott fortification plan. First mentioned by the Board in 1888, it was deemed “so successful” at Fort Wadsworth after installation in 1891 the Board installed another at Fort Hamilton in 1894. The Board then ordered three models for different elevations, which were sent in 1895–1896 to the U.S. Army coastal forts of San Francisco, Fort Monroe, and Fort Adams. By 1896 the Lewis was accurate within about 1 percent for distances under 10,000 yards and easy to read, proving superior to the Rafferty in 1897. Mechanical defects emerged by 1904, which the United States Rapid Fire Gun and Power Company attempted to redesign, but was not adopted as the in-service range finders were deemed sufficient. Maj. Isaac Newton Lewis updated the design of the rangefinder at Fort Monroe in 1906-1907. The detailed competitive test at Fort Wadsworth in October-November of 1907 showed the redesigned Lewis had the lowest average error (24.4 yards) compared with Warner & Swasey, Whistler-Hearn, and Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller instruments. The last mention of the Lewis range finder occurred in 1908 in which the Board decided to no longer delay required purchases. However, no funds were allotted after 1895 for additional purchases. Subsequent allocations post 1908 favored other models, such as Barr & Stroud, likely due to manufacturing advantages. The Navy appears never to have adopted the Lewis range finder.

body_sections.heading: Executive Summary (≤ 250 words)
lines: One short paragraph: what it is, why it matters to BOF, headline timeline (first BOF mention → last), and what ultimately became of the technology.

​​The Lewis range finder, a vertical-base depression instrument, measured the range and direction of moving targets as part of the updates to coastal defense in the Endicott fortification plan. First mentioned by the Board in 1888, it was deemed “so successful” at Fort Wadsworth after installation in 1891 the Board installed another at Fort Hamilton in 1894. The Board then ordered three models for different elevations, which were sent in 1895–1896 to the U.S. Army coastal forts of San Francisco, Fort Monroe, and Fort Adams. By 1896 the Lewis was accurate within about 1 percent for distances under 10,000 yards and easy to read, proving superior to the Rafferty in 1897. Mechanical defects emerged by 1904, which the United States Rapid Fire Gun and Power Company attempted to redesign, but was not adopted as the in-service range finders were deemed sufficient. Maj. Isaac Newton Lewis updated the design of the rangefinder at Fort Monroe in 1906-1907. The detailed competitive test at Fort Wadsworth in October-November of 1907 showed the redesigned Lewis had the lowest average error (24.4 yards) compared with Warner & Swasey, Whistler-Hearn, and Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller instruments. The last mention of the Lewis range finder occurred in 1908 in which the Board decided to no longer delay required purchases. However, no funds were allotted after 1895 for additional purchases. Subsequent allocations post 1908 favored other models, such as Barr & Stroud, likely due to manufacturing advantages. The Navy appears never to have adopted the Lewis range finder.

body_sections.heading: Timeline of Development & BOF Excerpts
lines: A stitched chronology combining BOF excerpts and context from other sources. Feel free to basically excerpt all complete mentions from the BOF annual reports, and then “fill in the blanks” of additional context as necessary with external sources. This should be the most detailed part of this memo. The entries can be long!
Date (YYYY or YYYY‑MM‑DD) Short Title of Event (e.g., BOF orders trials)
BOF Excerpt (with citation) [if from the annual report]
Additional Context & Analysis (supplement w/ external sources)

“With a view of testing all available forms of range finders which give promise of merit many have been allotted for by the Board and some of them have been received and their test is in progress. Two instruments for seacoast defence have been purchased, viz: The Fiske range and position finder, which is an instrument using a horizontal base, in which the horizontal triangle, with a base of about 277 yards, is solved electrically by the principle of the Wheatstrone bridge; and the Lewis range and position finder, in which the vertical triangle, the base of which is the height above the sea level, is solved mechanically. Both instruments are of delicate construction and not readily moved from one position to another” (BOF.AR.3.1892-1893, p.23)

The Board makes no direct mention during their discussion portion on range and position finders only noting that there “will soon result in the selection of a satisfactory instrument”.  (BOF.AR.4.1893-1894)
1893-1894 Proceedings of a Board of Officers to Test Range and Position Finders

“The Board proceeded to examine the Lewis range finder in its present position, 65 feet 2 inches above mean low water. The working of the enlisted men, the positions of the boat being simultaneously checked by observing parties at the extremities of the Hamilton-Wadsworth base line. NOTE.-- The Board appreciated that satisfactory results could hardly be expected, as the wind and waves being very high the light boat was tossed up and down, thus presenting no steady horizontal line on which to direct the range finer. The board is also satisfied that the system of signals adopted was so defective as not to result in observations entirely simultaneous” (BOF.AR.4.1893-1894 p.49).
Charts on range finders performance compared to others included
“Seacoast range finders. –Two new instruments of this class have been installed at Fort Hamilton, New York Harbor, during the year–the Fiske and the Lewis” (BOF.AR.5.1894-1895 p.16)

“The Lewis depression range finder.– The Lewis instrument at Fort Wadsworth was so successful that the Board recommended the purchase of another for Fort Hamilton, embodying such improvements as had been suggested to the inventor by the use of the other. The one ordered was of the first type–for altitudes less than 100 feet. The range finder was tested by the Range-Finder Board, who reported that it was well adapted for service. Before definitely recommending its adoption, the Board considered it wise to have three made, one of each of the types designed by the inventor, viz, for high elevations (200 to 400 feet), for medium elevations (100 to 200 feet), and for low elevations (30 to 100 feet), and to expose them to service conditions at representative artillery forts. An allotment was made for this purpose and the instruments will soon be ready for issue”  (BOF.AR.5.1894-1895 p.16)

“Lewis position finder, model of 1895.–Three types of this instrument were recommended to be made– one for heights between 30 and 100 feet, one for heights between 100 and 200 feet, and one for heights between 200 and 400 feet. An instrument representing the first type has been completed and set up at Fort Hamilton. The average height of telescope above water level is about 58 feet. As with the Fiske instrument there has not been sufficient time since completion to make any exhaustive tests to show what accuracy can be relied on these. These tests will be made during the coming season”  (BOF.AR.5.1894-1895 p.35)

“The Lewis depression position finder.--Upon the report of the Range Finder Board, and as the result of actual trial by the Board of Ordnance and Fortification, this instrument was recommended for adoption as the type for range finders having a vertical base. The recommendation of the Board was approved by the Secretary of War February 8 1896. It was also recommended that steps be taken to acquire the right to use this instrument upon such terms or at such rate of compensation as may, to the Secretary of War, seem just and equitable. This instrument takes the distance from its level to that of the water for its base, and, after proper adjustment, an observation on the water-line of a ship or other object by means of a powerful telescope directly on a dial distance in yards.The azimuth of the object is given by means of another dial as an arm in the vertical plane of the telescope moves on an iron table. The dials are very easily read, and experiment has shown the instrument to be accurate within about 1 percent for distances under 10,000 yards. The three instruments stated in the last report to have been ordered by the Board have been sent to San Francisco, Cal., Fort Monroe, Va., and Fort Adams R.I” (BOF.AR.6.1895-1896 p.923)
During this time the Board has still not recommended the adoption of a range finder using a horizontal base
1896-1897 Emergency range finder and inconclusive tests

“In addition to the principal range and position finders, placed at a distance from the guns, there should be others in the batteries themselves for use in case the communication with the range-finding station should be cut or the instrument should for any reason break down. In order to provide for this contingency, the Board to Test Range finders prepared the specifications for an emergency position finder. Under the advertisement issued, two different instruments were received – the Lewis and the Rafferty. The price of the latter being the lower, it was ordered, and upon testing it more than fulfilled the requirements. The Lewis was a portable modification of the larger instrument invented by Lieutenant Lewis, and, upon the application of that officer, this Board authorized a trial of it in competition with the Rafferty. The result of this test was not regarded as altogether conclusive, and it was decided to order both instruments for the typical artillery station at Fort Wadsworth, and to await the result of service use before recommending either type” (BOF.AR.7 1896-1897 P. 15)
1897 Lewis Range Finder Better than Rafferty

“Under the date of June 15, 1897, the board was directed to make further tests on the Lewis and Rafferty supplementary range finders with a view of determining which of the instruments is the better for service use. The board reported, August 13, 1897, that the Lewis supplementary depression position finder was the better for service use” (BOF.AR.7 1896-1897 P. 25).

“At its meeting April 12, 1898, the Board recommended that the instrument adopted as the emergency type position finder should have an accuracy of 1 per cent of range up to 6,000 yards when the vertical base is 60 feet; when greater than 60 feet, the accuracy should be 1 per cent of range up to 8,000 yards, and the instruments should be so constructed in several classes as to admit of being adjusted to varying lengths of base within certain limits. This proceeding was approved by the Secretary of War. Both the Rafferty and the Lewis instruments comply with these requirements, and the question as to which will be procured should be determined by the cost” (BOF.AR.8.1897-1898 p. 11).
1904 Continued Tests to Sort out Mechanical Defect

Modified Lewis depression position finder. –May 1, 1902, the Board made an allotment of $2,000 for the construction of a modified type of the Lewis depression range and position finder, certain mechanical defects having arisen in the continued use of the instrument in service. The United States Rapid Fire Gun and Power Company, the manufacturers of the instrument, submitted drawings of the modified design, which were approved by the Board, but under the date of February 13, requested an additional allotment of $1,440 for completing a sample instrument. March 3, 1904, the company was requested to submit their latest design, upon receipt of which the Board would consider the question of making further allotment” (BOF.AR.12. 1903-1904, p. 442).

“May 1, 1902, the Board made an allotment of $2,000 for the construction of a modified type of the Lewis depression range and position finder, certain mechanical defects having arisen in the continued use of the instrument in service. The United States Rapid Fire Gun and Power Company, the manufacturers of the instrument, submitted drawings of the modified design, which were approved by the Board, but under the date of February 13, requested an additional allotment of $1,440* for completing a sample instrument. March 3, 1904, the company was requested to submit their latest design, upon receipt of which the Board would consider the question of making further allotment. The design submitted was not considered by the Board equal to instruments already in service, and on May 4, 1905, the allotment was revoked. Under date of May 9, 1905, the company submitted a claim for $2,237. 45 to cover expenditures made in redesigning the instrument, which is now under consideration” (BOF. AR.15 1904-1905, p. 15).
*Can’t find the allotment of $1,440 they reference in any of the spreadsheets

““May 1, 1902, the Board made an allotment of $2,000 for the construction of a modified type of the Lewis depression range and position finder, certain mechanical defects having arisen in the continued use of the instrument in service. The United States Rapid Fire Gun and Power Company, the manufacturers of the instrument, submitted drawings of the modified design, which were approved by the Board, but under the date of February 13, requested an additional allotment of $1,440 for completing a sample instrument. March 3, 1904, the company was requested to submit their latest design, upon receipt of which the Board would consider the question of making further allotment. The design submitted was not considered by the Board equal to instruments already in service, and on May 4, 1905, the allotment was revoked. Under date of May 9, 1905, the company submitted a claim for $2,237.45 to cover expenditures made in redesigning the instrument, but this claim was later withdrawn and the incident closed” (BOF.AR.16. 1905-1906, p. 11)

“A new instrument, designed by Maj. I.N. Lewis, Coast Artillery Crops, was also tested at Fort Monroe. Other designers having signified their intention of submitting improved position finders, the Board has arranged to conduct a competitive test, beginning October 7, 1907, at Fort Wadsworth N.Y., where the facilities for testing a number of instruments are better than at Fort Monroe. The following instruments have been entered for this test, viz: The Warner & Swasey Company, the Whistler-Hearn, the Lewis, the Rafferty, and the Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller Company” (BOF.AR.17. 1906-1907, p.10)

“A competitive test of depression or vertical base range and position finders was held at Fort Wadsworth during October and November, 1907. Invitations to submit instruments were sent to all probable competitors and the following position finders: The Warner and Swasey, the Whistler-Hearn, the Lewis and the Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller. The test was conducted by the Artillery Board and was the most complete one of range finders that has ever been made in this country, more than 17,000 separate observations, at both fixed and moving targets, being made, recorded, and checked with simultaneous observations from horizontal base stations. The test included every essential feature necessary to determine the best coast range finder under the normal service conditions of use by officers and enlisted men of the Coast Artillery. The observations covered ranges from minimum of 2,000 yards to a maximum of 12,000 yards [...] at fixed points the average error of the several instruments for all the ranges was as followed: Lewis 24.4 yards, Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller 35.1 yards, Whistler-Hearn 38.0 yards, Warner & Swasey 55.8 yards [...] The supplemental test was completed November 11 and the Lewis instrument selected, so that the purchase of required instruments will not be longer be delayed” BOF.AR.18. 1907-1908 Range Finders p. 12)

Interesting note that Warner & Swasey protested the Board against the report that one paragraph of the specifications had not been carried out and therefore, a further test needs to be made to consider these specifications so no competitor can have a complaint (BOF.AR.18. 1907-1908 Range Finders p. 12)

In 1907 Rear Admiral Newton E Mason Chief of the Bureau of Ordnance Department complained that, “It will also probably be necessary to purchase range finders abroad, as so far American manufacturers have been unable to develop a satisfactory range finder for use on board ship, although several firms have been endeavoring to do so, without success at present” (https://archive.org/details/sim_armed-forces-journal_1907-11-30_45_13/page/320/mode/2up?q=%22Range-Finder%22)

Found that in 1908 in the army and navy journal that the “We learn that the Lewis Range Finder has successfully passed the mechanical tests to which it was subjected in response to complaints made by the owners of some other patent offered in competition with it” (https://archive.org/details/sim_armed-forces-journal_1908-08-29_45_52/page/1428/mode/2up?q=Lewis+%22Range-Finder%22)

body_sections.heading: Invention Assessment
lines: Answer as many of the following questions to the best of your ability in a few sentences. Can keep them as a bulleted list!
Why was there interest in this technology?
As part of the Endicott program initiated in 1885 to modernize harbor defenses a technology was needed to track a moving target, so that gunners could accurately know where to aim

Since the Lewis range-finder was a vertical range-finding technology this meant only one instrument was needed to calculate distance to the target as opposed to horizontal range finder which needed two points of observation and at least two people to record observations
It relied upon the instruments known height above the sea to calculate the angle of depression and accurately pinpoint the target

By 1895-1896 the Board found the Lewis dials are very “easily read, and experiment has shown the instrument to be accurate within about 1 percent for distances under 10,000 yards” (BOF.AR.6.1895-1896 p.923)
What other technologies were related to this one (did it competed with any or complement or improve upon other technologies)?
By 1895 the Lewis had been more extensively tested and the Board seems to support the design of vertical range finding more than horizontal
Both the Lewis and the Fiske were recorded as "delicate" and hard to move from one place to another
Vertical or Depression based Range finders in competition with the Lewis included: Warner & Swasey Company, the Whistler-Hearn, the Rafferty, and the Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller Company
In 1909 the Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller Company range finder had added modifications but did not improve after the test and was also approved for service use  (BOF.AR.20. 1908-1909 Range Finders p.7-8)
Interesting that most of the allotted money for range finders seems to go to this design even though the Lewis has the best final testing results

In 1897 the Lewis was found to be more efficient then the rafferty however, the next year they determined more testing needed to be done between the two to determine which is best for an emergency position finder
Efficacy or shortcomings based on trials/data?
Results from 1907-1908 competitive test average error at fixed ranges: Lewis 24.4 yards, Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller 35.1 yards, Whistler-Hearn 38.0 yards, Warner & Swasey 55.8 yards
Mechanical defects have arisen in 1902 with the Lewis instrument that is currently in service

From 1904-1906 the Board seems to be tracking allotments from the United States Rapid Fire Gun and Power Company which asked and was granted $2,000 for a modified range finder to fix the defect one however, this was revoked and no progress on improving the Range Finder was made until the 1906 competitive tests
The Lewis range finder seemed to work for the period 1896-1902 where the Board decided to retest more models to see if they could improve the accuracy of the technology
By the time they found better range finders it does not seem they were actively allotting money for the even though these tests were occurring
BOF funded in 1895 for three new instruments because by testing found they Lewis was the most effective compared to models at the time
Revisited once technology developed in 1906-1907 with more competitive tests where the new Lewis design fared the best
Ended up accepting this design and says purchases will no longer be delayed however, these purchases must have occurred outside the Board and within the Army Coast Artillery itself
Where did it go in the War Department after it was funded by the BOF, e.g. did the Army or Navy end up buying this technology at scale? Using this technology?
The Army had three range finders of different elevations sent to San Francisco, Cal., Fort Monroe, Va., and Fort Adams R.I in 1895-1896.
No more money for the Lewis was allotted after 1907 and instead other range finders and the board actually continues spending money on horizontal range finder testing
The navy never bought this technology
However, on a large scale there are no or few records I can find on using this technology on a larger scale.
In later documents the Board seems to be investing in range finders like the Barr & Stroud or the Bausch-Lomb-Saegmuller and not the Lewis
This could be because the manufacturing technology for the other ranger finders was more efficient since the company was more established
The Board’s extensive testing progress can slow down technological development.
The Navy never recognized the benefits of the range finder, even in 1907. The Board records that the technology had only been sent to three Army forts in 1896.
Only five range finders were officially funded by the Board of the Lewis type this doesn’t seem like a lot for the result of the tests and the time it took to conduct them

body_sections.heading: Details on Relevant People/Organizations
lines: Graduated from the U.S. Military Academy at West Point in 1884
Invented the Lewis machine gun in 1911, initially rejected by the U.S. Army because political differences between Lewis and General William Crozier
After retiring as a colonel in 1913, moved to Europe, established a factory in Belgium, and later relocated to England, merging with Birmingham Small Arms Company
Approximately 100,000 Lewis guns were produced and widely used by Allied forces in WWI, leading eventually to the U.S. Army adoption.

In subjects considered page 16 of the 9th report 1899-1900 no mention of lewis only proposal from the Board on Regulation of Sea Coast Artillery Fire for combination of horizontal and depression range finder which the comments say work discontinued and balance of allotment revoked: does this impact other range finding tests? Look into in the next report revoked allotment for this combination
BOF 13 allotted money for the rafferty but not the lewis (2,500) p. 388 also in the index says report on range and position finders is one page 248 but there is no 248
In 1903-1904 moved onto the Warner & Swasey range finder and the Bethlehem steel company horizontal base range finder invested in both 5,000 dollars
1903-1904 said made deposit of 2,000 didn’t even name lewis until later…
1905-1906 even with other range finders they are starting to get fed up with efficiency and will not make any allotments unless test results are satisfactory

After competitive test seem to go along with barr and stroud range finder in rest of documents in the 1908-1909 the Bausch, Lomb, Saegmuller company range finder according to the test in 1907 was recommended and now the instrument is in service use (BOF.AR.19 1908-1909 p. 9).

Most confused as to why the Lewis range finder tests were the best but no more mentions afterward tests or allocated funds. I cannot find anything in national records so far about the Army purchasing any more. I wonder if manufacturing, but also politics played a role in other rangefinders getting priority over the one Lewis designed.

summary: The Luger pistol is an automatic pistol invented by George Luger and distributed by the German arms manufacturer Deutsche Waffens. The BOF invested in the German Luger pistol as well as the American Colt pistol and the Borchardt Automatic Pistol Carbine to test automatic pistols to be supplied for the militia. The automatic pistols were tested to replace the revolver pistol for practical service purposes. The Luger pistol eventually became standard issue for most of the European army during the First World War, but it was not widely used by the American army and the Colt pistol became the American military’s standard issue pistol.

body_sections.heading: Timeline of Development & BOF Excerpts
lines: A stitched chronology combining BOF excerpts and context from other sources. Feel free to basically excerpt all complete mentions from the BOF annual reports, and then “fill in the blanks” of additional context as necessary with external sources. This should be the most detailed part of this memo. The entries can be long!
● Date (YYYY or YYYY‑MM‑DD) Short Title of Event (e.g., BOF orders trials) ○ BOF Excerpt (with citation) [if from the annual report]
○ Additional Context & Analysis (supplement w/ external sources)

○ Georg Luger, then working for Deutsche Waffens, developed a refined version of the semi-automatic pistol that his predecessor Borchardt originally developed. Much like his predecessor’s version, Luger’s version, called the Borchardt-Luger model, incorporated a refined version of the toggle-lock system unique to Borchardt’s design. Luger would patent his invention this year. (CAPITAL) ■ Source: Luxus Capital. “The Luger Pistol” November 4th, 2024.
● 1899-1900, initial test trials of Luger pistol by the Swiss Army

○ The refined Borchardt-Luger model became successful enough to be tested by the Swiss Army. After initial trials, the Swiss Army incorporated the pistol as its standard sidearm. The reliability of and accuracy of the Luger pistol would lead to other European nations adopting the pistol as its standard issue. (CAPITAL) ■ Source: Luxus Capital. “The Luger Pistol” November 4th, 2024.

● 1899, Philippine insurrection of 1899 and the demand for more powerful sidearms ○ During the Philippine insurrection of 1899, revolver pistols were found to be ineffective against enemy troopers. This motivated the US military to find a replacement to the standard revolver sidearm of the time. The main purpose of the replacement was to find a sidearm with greater stopping power than the standard issue Colt revolvers. (NRA)
■ Source: NRA American Rifleman. “The Trials of the M1911” May 24th 2011.
● 1901-04-06, BOF Luger Pistol Practical Service Test

○ “The Board at its meeting, April 6, 1901, made an allotment of $15,000 for the purchase from the German Arms and Ammunition Company of Berlin of 1,000 Luger automatic pistols for practical service test by troops in the field.

The preliminary firing tests of this arm at the Springfield Armory gave most satisfactory results. When received, these pistols will be distributed, five to each troops of cavalry in service and the remainder to the field artillery.
Commandering officers of organizations receiving these pistols will be instructed to make a report on the arm as the result of their tests, covering specifically the following points:
1. The advantages and disadvantages of automatic pistols as compared with the revolver.
2. Advantages and disadvantages of this particular arm as compared with the revolver.
3. Advantages and disadvantages of this pistol as compared with other automatic pistols.
4. Suitability of automatic pistols for the use of enlisted men.
5. If not deemed suitable for the use of the enlisted men as a whole, would it be advisable to issue them for the use of officers and noncommissioned officers?” (13-14, BOF.AR11.1900-1901)
● 1903-04-03, Luger Pistol Practical Service Test by Cavalry and Light Artillery Boards at Fort Riley

○ “At its meeting April 6, 1901, the Board made an allotment of $15,000 for the purchase from the Deutsche Waffen- und Munitions-Fabriken of Berlin of 1,000 Luger automatic pistols of 7.65-millimeter caliber, for practical service test in the field.

The preliminary tests at Springfield Armory gave most satisfactory results and when received the pistols were distributed to the cavalry and light artillery. Commanding officers of organizations were instructed to make a report on the arm. The reports received varied so widely in the expression of opinion as to the merits of the arm that the Board, on April 3, 1903, made provision for exchanging fifty of the 7.65-millimeter pistols for fifty of 9 millimeters with 3-⅞ -inch length of barrel, and when received they will be submitted to a final test by the cavalry and light artillery boards at Fort Riley to dotor-mine their suitability for adoption in the military service. On April 18, 1903, an allotment of $8,500 was made to procure ammunition for these tests, which it is expected will take place this fall.” (384, BOF.AR.13.1902-1903)

● Context: There were mixed reports of the Luger pistol test trials due to its deficient stopping power. The Luger pistols lacked the qualities that the US military was looking for in a pistol that had stronger stopping power than the then standard issue revolvers Later in 1904, the US Army also tested the later model of Luger and still found it to lack stopping power.
○ Source: The Armory Life. “Did This Almost Defeat the 1911?” April 1st, 2020. https://www.thearmorylife.com/did-this-almost-defeat-the-1911/
○ “At its meeting, April 6, 1901, the Board made an allotment of $15,000 for the purchase of 1,000 Luger automatic pistols of 7.65 millimeter caliber, for practical service test in the field.

The pistols were distributed to the cavalry and artillery with instructions to report on the arm. The reports varied so widely in the expression of opinion as to the merits of the arm that the Board, on April 3, 1903, arranged for exchanging fifty of the 7.65 millimeter for fifty 9 millimeter pistols, with barrels 3-⅞-inches long.
These pistols were sent to Fort Riley for test by the Cavalry and Field Artillery Boards.

A new model of the Colt automatic pistol having successfully passed the test at Springfield Armory, the Board, on January 11, 1902, made an allotment of $4,000 for the purchase of 200 of the latest model. These pistols were distributed to the service for practical test and report.

After considering the reports of the Cavalry and Field Artillery Boards by which the test was conducted, this Board in its proceedings of March 2, 1905, stated that “the adoption for use in the military service of either the Colt or Luger automatic pistols in the stage of development in which they have been tested is not recommended. The Board does not wish to be understood as condemning the automatic principle as such, but only as finding that its mechanical embodiment as presented for test does not give as satisfactory a pistol as the revolver.” (BOF.AR.15.1904-1905)

● Context: After the initial trials, the Ordnance wanted the pistols to have greater stopping power than the one they possessed at the time. This led to the Ordnance’s mandates to create a bigger bore for the pistols to house and fire larger rounds. This led to Luger creating prototype models of Luger pistols with bigger bores than the standard models produced.
○ Source: NRA American Rifleman. “The Trials of the M1911” May 24th 2011.
● 1908-04-16, Luger returns pistol orders from the Ordnance

○ Due to the increased competition for the replacement of standard issue sidearms, where Savage firearms became another competitor to Luger, Luger decided to opt out of the competition by returning his pistol orders from the Ordnance.
■ Source: Source: NRA American Rifleman. “The Trials of the M1911” May 24th 2011.
● 1911, Colt M1911 pistol trials and eventual adoption of the pistol

○ External Sources: After the Ordnance field tested the different models of Colt pistols multiple times, often with Savage’s pistols, the Ordnance eventually accepted the Colt pistol for standard issue. Colt’s pistol was found to be durable due to withstanding multiple firing tests without breaking, despite having a bigger bore than a regular pistol.
■ Source: NRA American Rifleman. “The Trials of the M1911” May 24th 2011.

● 1938, Walther P-38 replaces Luger P08 in Germany as standard issue sidearm ○ The German Army, Wehrmacht at the time, officially adopted the Walther P-38 model as the standard issue sidearm to replace Luger’s P08. Walther replaced Luger for its cost effective production scale. Luger required complex parts to be manufactured and had high costs for production. Walther P38 in contrast was cheaper and was easier to manufacture at scale. However, Luger was still used by officers of the Army.

body_sections.heading: Invention Assessment
lines: Answer as many of the following questions to the best of your ability in a few sentences. Can keep them as a bulleted list!
● Why was there interest in this technology?

○ Automatic pistols provided opportunities to improve upon the design of revolver pistols. Not only were they quicker to draw due to the automatic component of the pistol as well as their larger ammo capacities, they also provided greater opportunities to experiment with the pistol’s stopping powers. Although luger pistols did not end up having a satisfactory stopping power to replace the revolvers in the US, it was still widely used by most European nations during both World Wars due to its technological capabilities.
● What other technologies were related to this one (did it competed with any or complement or improve upon other technologies)?

○ Luger pistol’s competitor is often cited as the Colt M1911 pistol. They were both tested by the Board of Ordnance as well as the US Army for the purpose of replacing revolvers. Although most European nations preferred and distributed the Luger pistol during both World Wars, the Colt M1911 pistol was preferred by the US militia because it later was found to have greater stopping power than the Luger pistol.
● Efficacy or shortcomings based on trials/data?

○ There were shortcomings to the reliability of the Luger pistol during initial trials of the pistol. The Luger pistol, alongside the initial models of Colt pistols, were found to lack the firing power necessary to exceed the firing capacity of revolvers. Although the large capacity of the gun as well as its automatic firing capabilities were found to be desirable, this shortcoming ultimately made the Board of Ordnance reject Luger pistols.
○ Luger pistols were considered to be cost inefficient due to the complexity of its automatic system and the cost it required to produce and maintain it.
○ BOF declined to incorporate the Luger pistol into standard issue because it did not have the desired firing capacities the board was looking for.

● Where did it go in the War Department after it was funded by the BOF, e.g. did the Army or Navy end up buying this technology at scale? Using this technology? ○ The Army ended up buying the Colt M1911 model at scale later during 1908 after the Colt models were found to have satisfactory firing power in their field tests. The Luger pistol ended up being rejected by the Army and the Navy.

○ Long-term impact of the Luger pistol is that it eventually led to the creation of better, more cost-effective automatic pistols. This is evident with the adoption of German Walther P-38 pistols in 1938 as well as the US adoption of Colt pistols which were considered more cost-efficient in their production.

body_sections.heading: Open Questions & Next Steps
lines: This is a good place to keep track of any outstanding questions, notes to self, or anything you’re not sure about that you wanted to track.
● What were the comparative advantages of adopting a Luger as opposed to a Colt semi-automatic or revolver pistol as standard issue pistols?

● Was the Ordnance’s demand for having greater fire power, even at the expense of having to modify the pistol’s design to contain larger bores, a justified order? Did the M1911 model provide greater fire power that the Luger pistol did not provide?
● How ineffective was the Colt revolver during the Philippine insurrection of 1899? Were the revolvers ever produced to have stopping powers to resist an insurrection?

author: —
summary: With the industrial warfare of WW1 around the corner, European powers began replacing their fleets of horses as carriers of artillery and munitions with tracked vehicles. The U.S. was behind in this endeavor and, in 1912-13, the BOF attempted to hold trials for military tractors. However, no test ever materialized. Furthermore, the BOF repeatedly ignored the Holt tractor, arguably the best design which also served as the inspiration for the British Mark 1 tank. The main cause of this failure seems to be plain bureaucratic incompetence. The War Department finally adopted the Holt model in 1916, without the BOF’s input. The Holt company ended up selling 5,082 models to the U.S. and Entente governments and inspired Colonel Swinton to develop the Mark 1 tank.

body_sections.heading: Timeline of Development & BOF Excerpts
lines: 1913- Board begins plans to import foreign tractors for testing

BOF Excerpt: “Not a great deal of new work has been undertaken in the past year, but probably the most important of that undertaken has been the provision for determining the best type of motor tractor for military purposes, a subject heretofore neglected in this country.”
U.S. War Department, Report of the Board of Ordnance and Fortification for the Fiscal Year Ended June 30, 1913 (Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1913), 4.

BOF Excerpt: “One of the most important pieces of work undertaken by the board this year was the provision for an exhaustive test of motor tractors for military purposes. Very considerable attention has been paid to this subject by European Governments, but in this country practically all of the development of motor tractors has been for ordinary commercial purposes, and it is time consideration was given to the application of tractors to the problems of military transportation. One of the desirable uses to which tractors could be put would be for hauling the heavy siege guns and reserve ammunition for the artillery. This board has recommended the detail of a board of officers, to include the Field Artillery, Coast Artillery, Engineers, Ordnance Department, and Quartermaster Corps, to go thoroughly into the subject and make recommendations to the Board of Ordnance and Fortifications as the basis of further action and possible allotments. In order that the board of officers may have a definite basis of comparison, an allotment of $8,000 was made on February 6, 1913, for the purchase abroad of a motor tractor of the most advanced type developed for military purposes. On June 6, 1913, this sum was further increased $1,000, and on September 16 by $1,800, to cover additional purchase price, transportation, and test. The total allotment for the purpose is therefore $10,800.”
U.S. War Department, Report of the Board of Ordnance and Fortification for the Fiscal Year Ended June 30, 1913 (Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1913), 5–6.

Additional Context: A New York Tribune article, dated May 22, 1913, reports that the War Department has authorized tests on motor tractors at the urging of the Board. It does not state that the tests are ongoing, strongly implying that none of the tractors have yet arrived from Europe and that no tests have yet been undertaken.

Additional Context: “The development of motor transportation for military use is in its infancy in the United States. The first official attempt took place in 1903 when a combined battery and store wagon was constructed. Nothing has been done to determine the most advantageous types of vehicles until recent years. In 1912 an attempt was made by the Board of Ordnance and Fortification to have a tractor test. The initial step was interrupted by a recommendation that the test be delayed until a Panhard tractor could be purchased from France. That type of vehicle had been reported upon as producing remarkable results. Delay after delay ensued until finally the attempt was abandoned. In 1914 the War Department conducted the first real tests of motor trucks for military use. A Jeffery four-wheel drive car was tested at Fort Myer, but the first model did not come up to the requirements.”

Summary & Analysis: Sensing a lack of interest within the United States military for procuring motor tractors, which the Board viewed as vital to future artillery mobility and logistics, the Board attempted to organize a test of various tracked vehicles for military use in 1912. The test never happened, ostensibly due to delays of a French model, the Panhard, and most of the allotments were subsequently revoked. Along with the admission that “not a great deal of work had been undertaken,” this episode illustrates the bureaucratic quagmire the Board found itself in a short time before WW1. From 1912-1914, the Board undertook no other noteworthy procurement or research, and the U.S. had to adapt quickly and procure artillery tractors only after the start of the war in Europe.
1914- Test of Chatillon-Panhard and other tractor tests cancelled

Allotments aggregating $10,000 were made in 1913 for the purchase abroad of a motor tractor for military purposes. Considerable attention has been given to this subject by European Governments, but in this country practically all of the development of motor tractors has been for ordinary commercial purposes, and it was thought desirable to procure the most advanced type for test and observation in this country. Though the foreign manufacturers at first offered to sell a tractor, they finally declined to do so, but agreed to send one to the United States for test, and allotments were made on February 6 of $3,000 and on June 5, 1914, of $2,000 for transportation and all expenses of test of a Chatillon-Panhard tractor. Owing to the conditions abroad the manufacturers are now unable to furnish a tractor. All allotments have been revoked, and the subject will be taken up with manufacturers in this country.
(U.S. War Department, Report of the Board of Ordnance and Fortification for the Fiscal Year Ended June 30, 1914 (Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1914), 6–7.

Some time ago the board of ordnance and fortification made an allotment of $5,200, which later was increased to $10,500, to obtain from France a motor tractor of the Chatillon-Panhard type, for the purpose of tests to ascertain its suitability for use in our army for hauling field artillery and other heavy loads. There has been much delay and difficulty in making arrangements with the French manufacturers to secure the tractor, and such conditions have been imposed on the War Department that now it is regarded as futile to continue further negotiations for purchase of the vehicle. Therefore, it has been decided to accept the latest proposal from the French company to send a tractor to this country for the purpose of being tested in comparison with American vehicles. To this end, the previous allotments have been canceled, and a new one of $3,000 has been made to cover the cost of transportation and tests. Probably it will be seven or eight months before the tests can be held, and by that time it is believed that American motor tractors will be available for use under the observation of a board of army officers to be appointed for the purpose.

Analysis: After the failure of the Board to execute the 1912 test, ostensibly due to a delay in the delivery of the Chatilon-Panhard model, the Board allocated $3,000 for a new test in 1913. This allotment was later revoked and the test never happened. Despite 2 years of effort, the Chatillon never arrived and there is no evidence the Board ever tested any other tracked vehicles.

In 1904, Benjamin Holt and his four brothers produced the first Caterpillar trac-type farm tractor. The first test, on 24 November 1904, showed that the model, initially, steam-powered, could traverse hilly and muddy terrain.

In 1906, the Holt brother replaced the heavy and impractical steam-engine with a gasoline-powered internal-combustion engine. Subsequently, they began selling this model to the general public as agricultural machinery.

In 1909, the Holt brothers sold 27 models to the city of Los Angeles for the construction of an aqueduct in the Mojave Desert. The sale earned the company $141,000, improved its credibility, and the Caterpillar tractor a nationally recognized brand.

In 1911, the company sold a Caterpillar tractor to Dr. Leo Steiner, a Hungarian engineer/landowner, making it the first recorded international sale of the model. Steiner demonstrated the tractor to the Austro-Hungarian and German Armies as a vehicle for carrying artillery, however, both of these armies turned the tractor down.
1913-1916: Attempt at BOF consideration & Entente acquisition

In 1913-14 the company attempted to sell these tractors to the U.S. War Department as a vehicle to carry artillery and munitions. Ben Holt wrote a letter to Senator Miles Pointdexter, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Expenditures in the War Department, trying to interest the Army in testing and potentially buying the tractors. After delay, the Senator referred the letter to Major General Leonard Wood, president of the Board of Ordnance and Fortifications, who was not interested in the invention. In 1914, Holt again wrote to the BOF, saying that his company would fully pay for a test of their model at one of the BOF’s testing grounds. He was again ignored.
In 1914-1915, the governments of the UK, France and Russia had all bought large quantities of Holt Company’s tractors.
The War Department made its first test and began purchasing the tractors in 1915
Total sales of the tractor reached 1,000 by 1916 and 5,082 by 1918.
1916-1918:  Influence on the development of the tank

In 1914, the commonly designated inventor of the Mark 1 tank, British Colonel E. D. Swinton, received a letter from a friend, who witnessed a civilian Holt tractor in action in Antwerp. Swinton was reportedly inspired by the tractor in drafting his guidelines for the design of the Mark 1 tank

In 1916, the news of the Battle of the Somme caused a media sensation in the U.S. The similarity of the Mark 1 to the Holt caterpillar was so evident that after the news of this new invention reached the U.S., the media immediately linked it to the Caterpillar. When Holt returned from Europe to Stockton, California, he had a parade in his honor
In 1918, Swinton went to Stockton, where he gave a speech at a Holt factory. In this speech, he praised Holt for contributing to the invention of the tank and thereby to the allied war effort.
Wik, R. M. “The American Farm Tractor as Father of the Military Tank.” Agricultural History 54, no. 2 (1980): 283–90. https://www.jstor.org/stable/3742599.

body_sections.heading: Invention Assessment
lines: Answer as many of the following questions to the best of your ability in a few sentences. Can keep them as a bulleted list!
Why was there interest in this technology?

The motor tractor promised to offer a solution to the immense logistical problems of modern heavy artillery. Its carrying capacity and speed offered a strong alternative to horsepower, which was getting progressively less adequate to the task of moving the giant artillery pieces and munitions required for WW1.
What other technologies were related to this one (did it competed with any or complement or improve upon other technologies)?
The Holt company’s tractor directly inspired the British Mark 1 tank. The model’s tracked carriage and gasoline engine formed the basis for the British model.
Efficacy or shortcomings based on trials/data?

The BOF conducted ZERO trials of any tracked vehicles despite expressing interest in the model. In 1915, the War Department conducted its first test of the Holt model, which was met with great approval and immediately entered service.
The model was quite expensive, costing approximately $5,000 per unit. However, it proved so effective that both the War Department and Entente governments bought it en-masse.

The Board appears to have been bogged down in bureaucratic impasse, fist attempting to organize a competitive test for different models and then backtracking. The Holt Model was later tested and adopted by the War Department, however, there is no evidence of BOF participation in this process.
Where did it go in the War Department after it was funded by the BOF, e.g. did the Army or Navy end up buying this technology at scale? Using this technology?

Yes, after 1916 test  the War Department bought the Holt tractor en-masse. It was also sold to the British, French, and Russian governments. The total number of sold vehicles for military use was 5,082

After 1912, the Board appears to have been mostly lethargical and slow in adopting new technology. Despite allocating over $15,000 to the trial process of the tractor, no test was even held. This not only implies slowness, but outright waste, as the tests were continuously delayed until WW1 broke out, leaving the U.S. woefully unprepared for trench warfare. Finally, the War Department managed to purchase enough models by 1916, but without the BOF’s participation. The BOF utterly failed in its role of military innovation due to what appears to be plain bureaucratic incompetence.

lines: Holt Manufacturing Company- caterpillar tractor manufacturing company based in California owned by two brothers. Produced the Holt tractor and eventually merged with its main rival to form Caterpillar Inc., which exists to this day and is the world’s 229th most valuable company.

Major General Leonard Wood- Army Chief of Staff who was President of BOF when Senator Miles Pointdexter suggested adopting the tractor. Essentially refused to consider the project. Could have been caused by personal characteristics, rivalries, or general state of the BOF.
Senator Miles Pointdexter- Republican Senator from Washington and Chairman of the Senate Committee on War Department Expenditures. Relayed information on the Holt tractor to General Miles

Colonel E. D. Swinton- British Army Colonel widely recognized as the inventor of the Mark 1 tank, took inspiration from a letter sent by a friend describing a Holt Caterpillar's performance in Antwerp, Belgium.

body_sections.heading: Executive Summary (≤ 250 words)
lines: One short paragraph: Lyman-Haskell multi-charge gun is an experimental heavy ordnance artillery that incorporated multiple propellants to increase the velocity of the projectile. The design was initially created by Lyman with his patent on improvements in accelerating firearms in 1852. It was later developed by Haskell and demonstrated to the US Army Ordnance in 1880, where it was rejected due to functional and technical issues related to its large and heavy structure and inconvenient reloading mechanic. The Haskell multi-charge gun would ultimately serve as a base model that inspired the creation of German V3 cannons used during the Second World War. Haskell’s multi-charge gun once competed with Crozier’s wire-wound 10-inch BL Rifle, which had a practical and sturdier design compared to Haskell’s system.

each shot also made it so that the gun needed to be
reloaded for each time it was fired.
